content.rendered:
Arqiva occupies a unique and often misunderstood position in the United Kingdom’s telecommunications landscape. Unlike Vodafone, EE, or Three, Arqiva is not a consumer-facing mobile network. Its roots lie in the very foundations of British broadcasting — the transmitter network that has delivered television and radio signals to homes across the country for decades. If you are a landlord or landowner with an Arqiva installation on your property, your lease situation is likely to be quite different from that of a standard mobile phone mast, and understanding those differences is critical to protecting your interests.

The Arqiva story is one of transformation, privatisation, and corporate restructuring. Most significantly for landlords, Arqiva’s telecoms division was sold to Cellnex in 2019, meaning that many sites which were once managed by Arqiva are now under entirely different ownership. Whether your site remains with Arqiva as a broadcast installation or has been transferred to Cellnex as a telecoms asset, specialist surveying advice is essential to ensure you receive a fair rent and appropriate lease terms.

Arqiva: From BBC Transmitters to Modern Infrastructure

The history of Arqiva is inseparable from the history of British broadcasting itself. Understanding this lineage helps explain why Arqiva sites differ so markedly from conventional mobile mast installations.

Arqiva’s origins trace directly to the BBC’s transmitter network — the physical infrastructure of towers, masts, and transmission equipment that carried television and radio signals to every corner of the United Kingdom. For decades, this network was owned and operated by the BBC as an integral part of its public service broadcasting mandate.

In 1997, as part of a wider programme of privatisation, the BBC’s transmitter assets were sold to Crown Castle International, an American infrastructure company. Crown Castle operated these sites commercially whilst continuing to provide transmission services for the BBC and other broadcasters. Many landlords who host legacy broadcast towers will recall dealing with Crown Castle during this period.

The next change of ownership came in 2004, when National Grid plc — better known for its electricity and gas networks — purchased Crown Castle’s UK operations. National Grid saw synergies between its existing utility infrastructure and the telecoms tower business, and the acquisition brought these broadcast sites under the umbrella of one of Britain’s largest infrastructure companies.

In October 2005, the business was rebranded as National Grid Wireless, reflecting its new parentage. However, National Grid’s ownership proved relatively brief.

In April 2007, a consortium led by Arqiva (which was itself already a significant broadcast infrastructure company) purchased National Grid Wireless for £2.5 billion. This was a transformative deal that united the two largest broadcast transmission businesses in the UK under a single entity. The full amalgamation was completed in 2008, creating a combined operation with unrivalled coverage of the British broadcast transmission market.

Throughout the 2010s, Arqiva expanded beyond pure broadcast transmission into the wider telecoms infrastructure space, growing into a leading provider of both broadcast and mobile telecoms tower services. At its peak, Arqiva managed a diverse portfolio encompassing television transmission towers, radio broadcast sites, and thousands of mobile telecoms installations.

The decisive moment for many landlords came in October 2019, when Arqiva sold its entire telecoms division to Cellnex Telecom for £2 billion. This transaction transferred approximately 8,300 telecoms sites to Cellnex, the Spanish tower company that has since become the UK’s largest independent infrastructure provider. For landlords hosting telecoms installations, this meant a complete change of counterparty — from Arqiva to Cellnex — often without any direct consultation.

More recently, in June 2022, Digital 9 Infrastructure (a specialist digital infrastructure investment trust) acquired a 48% stake in Arqiva, bringing new investment into the business.

Today, Arqiva is a pure-play broadcast infrastructure provider, focused exclusively on television transmission, radio broadcasting, and satellite services. The company operates approximately 1,500 transmission sites across the United Kingdom. These are the sites where Arqiva remains the landlord’s lease counterparty — and they are quite distinct from standard mobile phone masts.

What Happened to Arqiva’s Telecoms Sites

This is the single most important distinction that landlords must understand: Arqiva no longer operates telecoms (mobile) sites. Every mobile telecoms installation that was previously managed by Arqiva was transferred to Cellnex as part of the 2019 sale.

If your site hosts mobile phone equipment — antennas, dishes, and cabinets serving the likes of Vodafone, EE, Three, or O2 — then your lease counterparty is almost certainly now Cellnex, regardless of what the original paperwork says. Cellnex stepped into Arqiva’s shoes and inherited all existing lease obligations and rights.

If your site is a broadcast transmission installation — a tower carrying television aerials, FM or DAB radio equipment, or satellite uplink facilities — then Arqiva remains your counterparty. These broadcast sites were not included in the Cellnex sale and continue to be owned and operated by Arqiva.

The confusion between these two categories is widespread, and we regularly speak with landlords who are unsure which type of site they host. In some cases, a single location may have historically served both broadcast and telecoms functions, adding further complexity. Our surveyors can help you determine exactly what type of installation you have and who your correct lease counterparty is.

Our Expertise with Arqiva and Legacy Broadcast Sites

At The Phone Mast Advice Company, we have worked with Arqiva landlords since the Crown Castle era. Our team of independent telecoms surveyors has witnessed every ownership change, every corporate restructuring, and every shift in the broadcast and telecoms infrastructure market over the past quarter of a century.

We are practised telecoms surveyors who have worked with Arqiva landlords since the Crown Castle days. We provide accurate rental assessments for both broadcast and legacy telecoms sites, negotiate lease renewals with a firm understanding of how Arqiva and Cellnex operate, and partner with telecom solicitors to see your lease through to legal completion.

Our independence is absolute: we act for landlords and landowners only, never for operators, tower companies, or infrastructure providers. This means our advice is always given with your financial and contractual interests as the sole priority.

Arqiva’s Broadcast Sites — Special Considerations for Landlords

If your site remains with Arqiva as a broadcast installation, there are several factors that distinguish it from a conventional mobile phone mast lease.

Scale and Physical Presence

Broadcast transmission towers are typically substantially larger structures than mobile phone masts. A standard mobile installation might involve a 15-metre monopole or a rooftop antenna array. A broadcast tower can stand 100 metres or more, with significant foundations, access roads, and compound areas. The physical footprint and visual impact of these structures is considerably greater, which is relevant to rental assessment.

Longevity of Installation

Many Arqiva broadcast sites have been in situ for decades. Some date back to the original BBC transmitter network established in the mid-twentieth century. This long-standing presence creates a unique dynamic: the infrastructure is deeply embedded, relocation would be extraordinarily costly and disruptive, and the site’s strategic importance to national broadcasting is significant. These factors should be reflected in the terms offered to landlords.

Regulatory and Licensing Framework

Broadcast transmission operates under a different regulatory framework from mobile telecommunications. Arqiva holds licences from Ofcom for the provision of broadcast multiplexes, and the regulatory requirements governing broadcast coverage create obligations that do not apply to mobile networks. This regulatory context influences both the commercial value of broadcast sites and the negotiating dynamics during lease renewals.

Rent Dynamics

The rental market for broadcast sites is distinct from the mobile mast market. Comparable evidence is more limited because there are far fewer broadcast towers than mobile installations. The strategic importance of individual broadcast sites, combined with the practical impossibility of relocation, creates a different value proposition. Landlords should not assume that broadcast site rents follow the same patterns as mobile mast rents — specialist assessment is essential.

Equipment and Maintenance Requirements

Broadcast installations typically involve more intensive maintenance regimes than mobile sites, with regular equipment upgrades to support evolving transmission standards (such as the transition from analogue to digital, or future changes in broadcast technology). Lease terms must adequately address access requirements, equipment specifications, and the landlord’s rights regarding modifications to the installation.

Frequently Asked Questions — Arqiva Phone Mast Lease

Is my Arqiva site a broadcast site or a telecoms site?

The simplest way to determine this is to look at the equipment on your site. Broadcast sites typically feature large transmission towers with television or radio aerials, whilst telecoms sites have smaller masts with panel antennas and microwave dishes serving mobile networks. If your site was transferred to Cellnex following the 2019 sale, you should have received correspondence confirming the change of counterparty. If you remain uncertain, our surveyors can inspect your site and confirm its classification — this is something we regularly assist landlords with.

My lease still says Arqiva but I’ve been contacted by Cellnex — what happened?

When Cellnex acquired Arqiva’s telecoms division in October 2019, all associated leases were transferred as part of the transaction. Your original lease document will still bear Arqiva’s name, but the legal counterparty is now Cellnex by virtue of the assignment. Most leases contain provisions permitting the operator to assign the lease without requiring the landlord’s consent. You should ensure that you have received formal notification of the assignment and that your records reflect the correct counterparty for future dealings, particularly rent payments and renewal correspondence.

Do broadcast mast sites command higher rents than mobile sites?

Not necessarily as a rule, but the factors affecting broadcast site value differ from those governing mobile mast rents. Broadcast towers are typically much larger, occupy more land, and have a greater visual impact. They are also strategically critical — a single broadcast tower may serve millions of television viewers, making relocation effectively impossible. These factors can support a strong rental position, but each site must be assessed individually. We provide detailed rental assessments that take all relevant factors into account for broadcast installations.

Can Arqiva install additional equipment on my broadcast tower?

This depends on the terms of your lease. Many broadcast site leases grant relatively broad rights for the operator to upgrade, modify, or add to the equipment on the tower, particularly where such changes are necessary to meet regulatory obligations or transition to new broadcast standards. However, the extent of these rights varies from lease to lease. If Arqiva has proposed installing additional equipment, we can review your lease to determine whether the proposed works fall within existing rights or whether your consent is required, potentially with an associated rental adjustment.

What is Digital 9 Infrastructure and does it affect my Arqiva lease?

Digital 9 Infrastructure is a specialist investment trust that acquired a 48% stake in Arqiva in June 2022. This investment brought additional capital into the business but does not directly alter the terms of your lease. Arqiva continues to operate as the same legal entity, and your lease counterparty remains unchanged. However, the involvement of institutional investors signals that Arqiva’s broadcast infrastructure is seen as a valuable long-term asset, which is a relevant consideration when assessing the commercial importance of your site during renewal negotiations.

How long do Arqiva lease renewals typically take?

Lease renewal timescales vary considerably depending on the complexity of the site, the extent of negotiations required, and whether legal issues arise. As a general guide, a straightforward broadcast site renewal might take six to twelve months from initial engagement to completed legal documentation. More complex cases — particularly those involving disputed rent levels, significant lease variations, or sites with multiple interests — can take longer. Our approach is to progress renewals efficiently whilst ensuring that no aspect of the negotiation is rushed to the detriment of your interests. We keep our clients informed at every stage and work closely with telecom solicitors to bring matters to a timely conclusion.

Whether Arqiva or Cellnex — We Can Advise You

Whether your site is a legacy Arqiva broadcast tower still operated by Arqiva, or a former Arqiva telecoms installation now managed by Cellnex, our team at The Phone Mast Advice Company has the specialist knowledge and negotiation experience to protect your interests.

We have advised landlords through every chapter of Arqiva’s corporate history — from Crown Castle through National Grid Wireless to the present day. We understand the broadcast market, the telecoms infrastructure market, and the critical differences between the two.

If you have an Arqiva site and want to understand your current position, or if you are approaching a lease renewal and need professional representation, we are here to help. We offer a free initial lease review to assess your situation and explain your options clearly.

Call us on 01691 791543 or complete our enquiry form to arrange your free consultation. With over 25 years of specialist experience and offices in London, Bristol, and Oswestry, The Phone Mast Advice Company is the trusted choice for UK landlords with broadcast and telecoms lease matters.

If you are a landlord or landowner with a telecommunications mast on your property, there is a growing chance that your lease counterparty is no longer the mobile operator you originally signed with. Instead, you may find yourself dealing with Cellnex Telecom — a name that many UK landlords had never heard of until a letter arrived informing them that their lease had been transferred. Understanding who Cellnex is, how they operate, and what this means for your rental income and contractual rights is essential before you agree to any lease renewal or variation.

Cellnex is not a mobile network operator. It does not provide phone signals to consumers. It is Europe’s largest independent telecommunications tower company, and its business model is fundamentally different from that of Vodafone, O2, Three, or EE. That distinction matters enormously when it comes to negotiating your mast lease, and it is precisely why landlords need specialist advice from surveyors who understand the tower company model inside and out.

Cellnex: Europe’s Tower Giant Arrives in the UK

To understand your current lease situation, it helps to know how Cellnex reached its dominant position in the British telecoms infrastructure market. The company’s rise has been rapid, strategic, and transformative for UK landlords.

Cellnex Telecom originated as a subsidiary of Abertis Telecom, a Spanish infrastructure group. The company was established as an independent entity and listed on the Barcelona Stock Exchange in 2015, with a clear mandate: to become Europe’s leading independent wireless infrastructure provider through rapid expansion through acquisition.

The UK entry came in 2016, when Cellnex acquired assets from Shere Group, a relatively small UK tower operator. This initial foothold was modest, but it signalled serious intent.

The transformative moment arrived in October 2019, when Cellnex completed its acquisition of Arqiva’s entire telecoms division — approximately 8,300 sites — for £2 billion. Overnight, thousands of UK landlords who had signed leases with Arqiva found themselves dealing with a Spanish-headquartered tower company they had never encountered before.

Cellnex moved again in June 2020, forging a landmark strategic alliance with BT/EE. Under this arrangement, Cellnex acquired rights to 220 high-rise towers, took ownership of 7,400 EE sites, and secured marketing rights for a further 900 locations. This single deal made Cellnex one of the most significant infrastructure players in British telecommunications.

In 2021, Cellnex acquired CK Hutchison’s UK tower assets — the infrastructure arm behind the Three mobile network — adding roughly 6,600 further sites to its UK portfolio. However, the scale of this combined acquisition attracted scrutiny from the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), which in May 2022 required Cellnex to divest approximately 1,100 sites to Wireless Infrastructure Group (WIG) as a condition of approval.

More recently, in 2024, Cellnex extended its co-location agreements with VMO2 (Virgin Media O2) and Vodafone, covering over 4,500 sites. These arrangements allow multiple operators to share Cellnex-owned infrastructure, further consolidating the company’s position.

Today, Cellnex operates approximately 14,500 telecommunications towers across the United Kingdom, making it by far the largest independent tower company in the country. If you have a mast on your land, there is a significant probability that Cellnex is now your lease counterparty — even if you have never signed a document with them directly.

The Tower Company Model — How Cellnex Operates Differently

The distinction between a mobile network operator and an independent tower company is not merely corporate semantics. It has direct, practical consequences for your lease and your rental income.

Mobile operators such as Vodafone, EE, and Three build and maintain masts primarily to serve their own network coverage requirements. When they lease a site on your land, the relationship is relatively straightforward: one operator, one mast, one lease.

Cellnex operates a neutral host model. Its entire business is built on owning tower infrastructure and leasing capacity to multiple mobile operators simultaneously. A single Cellnex tower might host equipment for two, three, or even four different operators. Each of those operators pays Cellnex for access, but the landlord typically has just one lease — with Cellnex.

This multi-tenant approach is central to Cellnex’s profitability. The more operators sharing a single tower, the greater Cellnex’s revenue from that site, whilst the rent paid to the landlord often remains fixed under the original lease terms. For landlords, this raises important questions about whether your rent reflects the true commercial value that Cellnex derives from your site.

Many landlords discover that their lease was originally signed with a mobile operator — perhaps Arqiva, Three, or EE — and has subsequently been assigned or novated to Cellnex as part of one of the acquisitions described above. You may not have been consulted about this transfer. In many cases, the lease permits assignment without the landlord’s consent, meaning Cellnex can step into the operator’s shoes and inherit all existing lease terms.

This is precisely why professional advice matters. The lease you signed a decade ago with a mobile operator may now be serving a fundamentally different commercial purpose under Cellnex’s ownership, and your next renewal is the opportunity to ensure your terms reflect that reality.

Our Approach to Cellnex Lease Matters

At The Phone Mast Advice Company, we are independent telecoms surveyors with over 25 years of experience advising landlords and landowners across the United Kingdom. We act exclusively for landlords — never for operators or tower companies — which means our advice is always aligned with your interests.

As independent telecoms surveyors, we bring deep market knowledge to Cellnex lease negotiations. We prepare detailed rental assessments reflecting current market conditions, negotiate every contractual clause during renewal discussions, and engage specialist telecom solicitors to handle the legal mechanics of lease completion.

Our team understands the nuances of the tower company model. We know how Cellnex values sites internally, how multi-tenancy affects commercial calculations, and where there is scope to achieve better terms for landlords. Whether your lease was originally signed with Arqiva, Three, EE, or another operator, we can advise on the specific implications of Cellnex’s involvement and ensure you are not left at a disadvantage.

With offices in London, Bristol, and Oswestry, we advise landlords across England, Scotland, and Wales.

Key Cellnex Lease Considerations for Landlords

There are several factors that make Cellnex leases distinct from traditional operator agreements, and landlords should be aware of each before entering renewal negotiations.

Long-Term Master Service Agreements

Cellnex typically operates under take-or-pay master service agreements (MSAs) with mobile operators, often spanning 15 to 20 years. These long-term commitments underpin Cellnex’s revenue certainty, but they can also influence the terms offered to landlords. Understanding how your individual site fits within Cellnex’s broader MSA framework is important when assessing rental proposals.

Rent Escalation Mechanisms

Cellnex leases frequently include CPI-linked (Consumer Price Index) or fixed annual escalators for rent increases. While index-linked reviews may appear attractive, the base rent from which escalation begins must be fair and reflective of current market value. An escalator applied to an undervalued base rent will leave you behind the market for the entirety of the lease term.

Multi-Tenant Revenue and Site Sharing

As noted above, Cellnex’s neutral host model means your site may accommodate multiple operators. Each additional tenant generates revenue for Cellnex, but the question of whether the landlord benefits from this additional commercial activity depends entirely on the lease terms. Some leases include site-sharing uplift provisions; many do not. If your current lease is silent on multi-tenancy, a renewal is the opportunity to address this.

Lease Assignment and Future Transfers

Cellnex has grown through acquisition, and the telecoms infrastructure market continues to evolve. There is no guarantee that Cellnex will remain your counterparty indefinitely. The terms governing lease assignment — whether Cellnex can transfer your lease to yet another company without your consent — should be carefully reviewed during any renewal.

Code Rights Under the Electronic Communications Code

Like all operators and infrastructure providers, Cellnex benefits from rights under the Electronic Communications Code (as amended in 2017). These statutory provisions significantly affect how telecoms leases are valued and how disputes are resolved. Professional advice is essential to ensure that your position is properly protected within this legislative framework.

Frequently Asked Questions — Cellnex Phone Mast Lease

My lease was originally with Arqiva — why is Cellnex now my counterparty?

In October 2019, Cellnex acquired Arqiva’s entire telecoms division, comprising approximately 8,300 sites across the UK. As part of that transaction, all associated leases were transferred to Cellnex. If your site was a telecoms (mobile) site managed by Arqiva, your lease counterparty is now Cellnex. This transfer was typically permitted under the assignment provisions of your original lease, which is why you may not have been asked to consent. It is important to review your current position with a specialist surveyor to ensure your terms remain appropriate under Cellnex’s management.

Does Cellnex pay different rents than mobile operators?

Cellnex operates a different business model from mobile operators, but the rent payable under your lease is governed by the terms of that lease and by prevailing market conditions. Tower companies and mobile operators both reference comparable evidence when assessing site value. However, the commercial dynamics differ because Cellnex generates multi-tenant revenue from sites, which can influence negotiation strategy. Our role is to ensure that the rent you receive properly reflects the value of your site in the current market.

Can Cellnex add more tenants to my mast without additional rent?

This depends entirely on your lease terms. Some older leases grant broad rights for the operator (now Cellnex) to share the site with other parties without requiring additional payment to the landlord. Other leases include specific site-sharing provisions that trigger a rental uplift when additional tenants are added. We review your lease carefully to identify whether you are entitled to additional rent when Cellnex introduces further operators to your site, and if the lease is silent on this point, we address it during renewal negotiations.

How do Cellnex’s long-term master service agreements affect my lease?

Cellnex enters into master service agreements with mobile operators that can span 15 to 20 years. These agreements provide revenue certainty for Cellnex but do not directly alter your lease terms. However, the existence of these MSAs means Cellnex has a strong commercial interest in retaining your site for the long term, which can be a relevant factor during negotiations. Our surveyors take Cellnex’s broader commercial position into account when advising on renewal strategy.

What happens if Cellnex sells my site to another tower company?

The telecoms infrastructure market is dynamic, and further consolidation or divestment is always possible. If Cellnex were to sell your site, your lease would transfer to the new owner along with all its existing terms. The key concern for landlords is ensuring that your lease contains appropriate protections regarding assignment, so that any future transfer does not leave you with an unknown counterparty and no recourse. This is something we address as a matter of course during lease renewal discussions.

Should I renegotiate my lease now that Cellnex has taken over?

If your lease is approaching renewal or if you have received correspondence from Cellnex regarding new terms, it is certainly the right time to seek professional advice. Even if your lease has some time remaining, understanding your current position — particularly regarding rent levels, site-sharing provisions, and assignment clauses — is valuable preparation. We offer a free initial consultation to assess your situation and explain your options. Contact us on 01691 791543 to arrange a discussion.

Get Specialist Advice on Your Cellnex Lease

Cellnex operates differently from traditional mobile operators, and landlords who approach renewal negotiations without understanding the tower company model risk leaving value on the table. At The Phone Mast Advice Company, we have the specialist market knowledge and negotiation experience to ensure your interests are properly represented.

Whether you have just discovered that Cellnex has taken over your lease, or you are preparing for an upcoming renewal, our team is ready to help. We provide a free initial consultation to review your lease position and advise on the best way forward.

Call us today on 01691 791543 or use our contact form to arrange your free consultation. With offices in London, Bristol, and Oswestry, we advise landlords across the whole of the United Kingdom.

The landscape for Three phone mast leases has shifted more dramatically in the past two years than at any point since the network launched. The completion of the Vodafone-Three merger in June 2025 created the UK’s largest mobile operator by subscriber count, and the consequences for landlords hosting Three masts are only beginning to unfold. If you have a Three installation on your property — whether a lattice mast, monopole, or rooftop antennas — the decisions you make about your lease in the coming months could define your commercial position for the next decade.

The Phone Mast Advice Company acts solely for landlords. We have never worked on behalf of any mobile operator, and that independence is the foundation of our advice. With more than 25 years as specialist telecoms surveyors, we have steered landlords through every significant restructuring event in British mobile telecommunications. The Vodafone-Three merger is the most consequential since the formation of EE, and we are already advising clients on its implications.

Three UK: The Challenger That Changed the Market

In March 2003, Hutchison Whampoa (now CK Hutchison Holdings) launched Three as the UK’s first network built exclusively on 3G technology. At a time when established operators were cautiously planning 3G upgrades, Three bet everything on the new standard. The gamble paid off — Three’s commitment to data-centric mobile services positioned it ahead of the curve as smartphone adoption accelerated.

By December 2004, Three became the first UK operator to meet Ofcom’s demanding 80 per cent population coverage requirement for 3G. To achieve this, CK Hutchison invested heavily in mast infrastructure, entering into thousands of lease agreements. Many of those original leases remain in force today, drafted in a market that bears little resemblance to the current one.

Between 2012 and 2013, Three secured valuable 800 MHz spectrum at Ofcom’s auction, enabling a limited 4G rollout. This lower-frequency spectrum was particularly useful for extending coverage into buildings and across rural areas, prompting site upgrades and new installations.

In March 2015, CK Hutchison made an audacious £10.25 billion bid for O2. The European Commission blocked the acquisition in May 2016 on competition grounds, concluding it would lead to higher prices and reduced consumer choice.

Undeterred, CK Hutchison turned to Vodafone. In June 2023, the two companies announced a merger of their UK operations. After extensive investigation, the Competition and Markets Authority approved the deal in December 2024, subject to commitments including an £11 billion network investment pledge. The merger completed in June 2025, creating an entity where Vodafone holds 51 per cent and CK Hutchison 49 per cent.

What the Vodafone-Three Merger Means for Landlords

This merger is not simply a change of corporate name on your lease. It is a fundamental restructuring with direct consequences for every landlord hosting a Three mast.

Network consolidation and potential decommissioning. Before the merger, Three and Vodafone each maintained independent mast networks. In many locations, both operators had separate installations — sometimes on adjacent properties. The merged entity has a powerful incentive to rationalise this estate, retaining the best-placed sites and decommissioning those that are redundant. If your Three mast sits in an area where Vodafone also has infrastructure, your site may face decommissioning risk.

Changes in counterparty. Your lease names Three (or Hutchison 3G UK Limited) as tenant. Following the merger, responsibility may transfer through formal assignment, novation, or gradual corporate integration. Understanding who your counterparty is — and who it may become — matters enormously when negotiating terms or asserting rights.

Greater negotiating power. A merged Vodafone-Three has substantially more leverage than Three alone. The combined company controls more sites, has more relocation options, and can deploy the resources of two major corporations in any negotiation. Landlords who dealt with Three as a mid-sized challenger will now face the UK’s largest mobile network company.

The £11 billion investment commitment. The CMA’s approval was contingent upon an £11 billion infrastructure investment. This programme involves upgrading existing sites, building new ones, and deploying 5G more widely. For some landlords, this is an opportunity — your site may be earmarked for upgrades. For others, the operator can point to alternative sites when negotiating rent.

Our Role in Navigating Three’s New Landscape

We are specialist telecoms surveyors who have guided landlords through every major UK network merger. We provide independent rental valuations based on current market evidence and the Electronic Communications Code. We negotiate the fine detail of contractual terms during lease renewals with Three and its successors, and instruct specialist telecom solicitors — not general practitioners, but lawyers who work with mast leases daily — to manage your lease to legal completion.

In the context of this merger, we bring particular value because we understand the strategic calculations the merged entity is making about its combined estate. We know which site types are most likely retained, which are vulnerable to decommissioning, and where genuine opportunities lie for landlords. This is informed analysis built on a quarter-century of experience.

Three’s Lease Approach — Before and After the Merger

Before the merger, Three operated as the smallest of the UK’s four operators. Its property team was lean, and its approach to renewals was often more straightforward. Three’s leases tended to be simpler in structure, with fewer sharing and assignment provisions and a more direct landlord-operator relationship.

Post-merger, everything is changing. The combined estate encompasses tens of thousands of sites from both networks. Property management is being consolidated, and landlords increasingly deal with a larger, more procedure-driven organisation. Renewals may take longer, involve more approval layers, and follow internal benchmarks developed for Vodafone’s much larger estate.

Site rationalisation is the critical question. Where both networks maintained separate masts in the same coverage area, the merged company will consolidate onto the better site. If yours is retained, your position may strengthen — the merged company needs your property for the combined subscriber base. If yours is earmarked for release, you face a different situation requiring careful handling.

Protecting Your Interests During Network Consolidation

Regardless of where you are in your lease cycle, here is what we recommend.

Review your existing lease. Understand its terms — particularly assignment, termination, and compensation provisions. If you cannot locate your lease, we can assist through Land Registry searches and direct operator enquiries.

Assess your site’s strategic value. A site providing unique coverage where Vodafone had no previous presence is far more valuable than one duplicating existing infrastructure. We can evaluate your site’s importance and advise how this affects your position.

Do not sign anything without specialist advice. Whether the merged company approaches you about a renewal, variation, or termination, do not respond without taking advice. Initial proposals are almost never the best terms available. Once signed, your negotiating position is gone.

Be alert to formal notices. Under the Electronic Communications Code, operators must serve formal notices to end agreements or impose new terms. These have strict procedural requirements and time limits. Any formal correspondence from Three, Vodafone, or their solicitors should be treated as urgent.

Consider your long-term objectives. Uncertainty cuts both ways. If the merged company needs your site, you may be stronger than you realise. If your site is surplus, early engagement can sometimes secure a better outcome than waiting for notice. We help landlords assess options realistically.

Frequently Asked Questions About Three Phone Mast Leases

Will my Three lease transfer to Vodafone after the merger?

The merger does not automatically transfer your lease. How your specific agreement is affected depends on its terms and the company’s integration plans. Some leases may be formally assigned to the new entity; others may remain with Three’s corporate vehicle for now. We can review your lease and advise on the most likely scenario.

Could my Three mast be decommissioned due to network overlap with Vodafone?

This is a genuine possibility, particularly where both networks had separate infrastructure in the same area. The merged company has strong financial incentive to eliminate duplication. However, the operator must follow Electronic Communications Code procedures, and you may be entitled to compensation. We can assess the risk to your specific site.

Should I sign a new lease now or wait until the merger settles?

This depends entirely on your circumstances. Securing a renewal before the merged company consolidates its estate can sometimes be advantageous. In other cases, waiting allows negotiation from greater knowledge of the company’s plans. There is no universal answer — we assess each case on its merits.

Who is my actual counterparty — Three, Vodafone, or a new entity?

Most existing Three leases remain with Hutchison 3G UK Limited or associated companies. As corporate integration progresses, this may change. Your counterparty’s identity determines who pays rent, who exercises lease rights, and who you deal with in disputes. We track the restructuring closely.

Can the merged company reduce my rent using the Electronic Communications Code?

The Code provides a framework for determining rent, and since the 2017 amendments, assessments have generally produced lower figures than historical levels. The merged company has the same Code rights as Three did independently. However, the Code also protects landlords, and rent must reflect your site’s specific circumstances. We ensure fair valuations that account for all relevant factors.

What compensation am I entitled to if my Three mast is removed?

If the operator terminates your lease and removes equipment, it must follow Code procedures. Compensation depends on your lease terms, remaining term, the rent received, and any loss or damage from removal. The amount is not standardised. We advise on entitlement and negotiate to ensure appropriate compensation.

Act Now to Protect Your Three Phone Mast Lease

The Vodafone-Three merger creates both risks and opportunities for landlords. Sites strategically important to the merged network may become more valuable; those overlapping with Vodafone infrastructure may face pressure. The merged company will have teams of professional advisers optimising its estate — you deserve the same level of expertise on your side.

Contact The Phone Mast Advice Company today on 01691 791543 for a free, no-obligation assessment of your Three lease. With offices in London, Bristol, and Oswestry and a track record spanning more than 25 years, we are the landlord’s specialist in an industry that has never been more complex. Do not wait for a notice to arrive — by then, the clock is already ticking.

EE commands the largest mobile network footprint in the United Kingdom, with 4G coverage reaching roughly 99 per cent of the population and 5G expanding rapidly. If you host an EE phone mast, your lease sits within one of the most complex corporate structures in British telecoms. The relationship between EE, its parent company BT Group, and infrastructure partner Cellnex means that understanding who you are dealing with — and what your rights are — requires genuine specialist knowledge.

At The Phone Mast Advice Company, we act exclusively for landlords. We have never represented a mobile operator, and we never shall. With over 25 years as specialist telecoms surveyors, we assess your rent independently, negotiate the contractual terms of your lease renewal, and work alongside specialist telecom solicitors who handle legal completion.

EE: From Orange and T-Mobile to BT Group

To understand your EE lease properly, you need to appreciate the corporate journey that brought EE into existence — a journey that left its mark on thousands of mast leases across the country.

Between 2004 and 2009, Orange UK (owned by France Telecom) and T-Mobile UK (a Deutsche Telekom subsidiary) operated as entirely separate networks, each acquiring sites and negotiating leases independently. If your lease dates from this era, it will bear one of these names and reflect the market conditions of the time.

In April 2010, Deutsche Telekom and France Telecom merged their UK operations into a 50:50 joint venture called Everything Everywhere Limited — the largest structural change in UK mobile telecoms since the launch of 3G. The combined entity inherited thousands of mast sites and their associated lease agreements from both operators.

By 2012, Everything Everywhere had rebranded as EE and launched the UK’s first commercial 4G network. The 4G rollout demanded significant investment in new equipment and upgrades to existing sites, prompting fresh lease negotiations with many landlords.

In December 2014, BT Group entered exclusive negotiations to acquire EE for approximately £12.5 billion. The Competition and Markets Authority approved the deal, and BT completed the acquisition in January 2016. EE became a wholly owned BT subsidiary, and lease management increasingly fell under BT’s substantial property and estates division.

Two further developments now affect landlords directly. In 2020, EE became a key participant in the Shared Rural Network, committing to deploy over 1,600 new masts in underserved rural areas. In June 2020, EE entered a strategic alliance with Cellnex encompassing approximately 220 high-rise towers, 7,400 existing sites, and 900 marketing rights for future installations — introducing a third party into what was previously a bilateral landlord-operator relationship.

EE’s Mast Network and BT’s Infrastructure

BT’s ownership brought together two of the largest telecoms infrastructure portfolios in the UK. For landlords, this means negotiating with a counterparty that has unparalleled knowledge of the property market and significant in-house resources. BT’s property team manages tens of thousands of sites, employing specialist lease managers and panels of solicitors who operate according to structured internal procedures.

The Cellnex partnership adds another layer. Where BT has transferred site management to Cellnex, your primary point of contact may have changed. Cellnex operates as a neutral infrastructure provider hosting equipment from multiple operators. If your site falls under Cellnex management, you need to understand how this affects your lease terms, rent review mechanism, and renewal rights.

The Shared Rural Network is generating entirely new lease agreements in areas that previously lacked coverage. If you have been approached about hosting a new EE mast under this programme, the terms on offer may differ significantly from those at established urban sites, with different access arrangements, equipment specifications, and commercial dynamics.

Our Expertise with EE Leases

As seasoned telecoms surveyors, we deliver thorough rental assessments for EE sites, handle all aspects of contractual negotiation during lease renewals, and coordinate with specialist telecom solicitors who manage the legal completion of your lease. We have acted for landlords across every type of site — from rooftop antennas on residential blocks to large-scale ground-based installations on agricultural land.

Our particular strength lies in understanding the BT corporate structure and its implications for landlords. We know how BT’s property team operates, what their internal benchmarking looks like, and where genuine room for negotiation exists. We have experience with leases originating under Orange, T-Mobile, and EE itself, and understand the subtle differences in drafting, rent review clauses, and termination provisions that distinguish these legacy agreements.

Challenges Landlords Face with EE

BT’s negotiating leverage. BT maintains one of the largest property portfolios in the UK, giving its lease team considerable expertise and leverage. They understand the Electronic Communications Code thoroughly and have resources to pursue protracted negotiations. An individual landlord without specialist support is at a material disadvantage.

Legacy lease complexity. If your lease originated with Orange or T-Mobile, you may be dealing with terms drafted under different market conditions and a different legal framework. Some legacy leases contain rent review mechanisms or assignment provisions that no longer reflect current best practice. Understanding what you have inherited requires careful analysis.

The Cellnex factor. Where management has transferred to Cellnex, landlords sometimes find themselves uncertain about their actual counterparty. Cellnex may manage the site daily, but the lease may still name EE or BT as tenant, creating confusion about responsibility for rent payments, equipment rights, and notices.

Urban versus rural dynamics. A rooftop antenna in central Manchester is a fundamentally different proposition from a ground-based mast in mid-Wales. We ensure that rental assessments and contractual terms reflect your specific site characteristics.

What to Expect During an EE Lease Renewal

Notification. EE or its agent serves formal notice regarding the lease. Under the Electronic Communications Code, operators must follow specific procedures. The notice’s timing and content matter — do not assume everything the operator sends is correct simply because it comes from a large company.

Independent rental assessment. We carry out a thorough valuation considering location, equipment type and extent, sharing arrangements, comparable evidence, and the Code’s provisions governing rent calculation.

Contractual negotiation. Rent is only one element. Access rights, equipment specifications, assignment provisions, break clauses, and landlord protections are equally important. We negotiate these terms drawing on our experience of what is reasonable in today’s market.

Solicitor involvement. Once commercial terms are agreed, specialist telecom solicitors draft and finalise the documentation. This work should never be left to a general high-street solicitor unfamiliar with mast leases.

Completion. We remain involved to ensure negotiated terms are accurately reflected in final documentation, with no detrimental last-minute changes.

Frequently Asked Questions About EE Phone Mast Leases

My lease is with Orange or T-Mobile — does EE still honour it?

Yes. When Everything Everywhere was formed in 2010, it assumed the obligations of both Orange UK and T-Mobile UK, including existing mast leases. Those leases remain valid and enforceable. However, the terms may be outdated, and a renewal provides an opportunity to modernise them to reflect current conditions.

How does BT’s ownership of EE affect my mast lease terms?

BT’s 2016 acquisition did not automatically change existing lease terms. However, renewals are now conducted by BT’s experienced lease management team, which tends to adopt a more structured and assertive approach than EE did independently, making specialist advice particularly valuable.

Can EE transfer my lease to Cellnex?

This depends on your agreement’s specific provisions. Many modern telecoms leases contain assignment clauses permitting the operator to transfer its interest to a tower company like Cellnex, potentially without your consent. If your lease lacks such a clause, your agreement would be needed. We can review your position.

What is the Shared Rural Network and could it affect my site?

The Shared Rural Network is a government-backed programme launched in 2020 to extend 4G coverage to underserved rural areas. EE is one of four participating operators. If your land is in a rural area, you may be approached about hosting new equipment. The terms offered may differ from standard commercial agreements.

What rent should I expect for an EE mast in 2026?

Rents vary enormously depending on location, site type, equipment extent, and the Electronic Communications Code. Since the 2017 Code reforms, rents have generally been assessed differently from historical levels. We provide site-specific valuations based on current comparable evidence. Quoting a generic figure without understanding your circumstances would be potentially unclear.

Do I need separate advice for rooftop EE equipment versus a ground-level mast?

The principles are the same, but practical considerations differ. Rooftop installations involve structural loading, communal access, aesthetic impact, and building services. Ground-level masts raise questions about land use, planning conditions, access routes, and compound footprint. We tailor our approach to your specific site.

Speak to Our Team About Your EE Phone Mast Lease

Whether your lease dates back to the Orange era, the T-Mobile years, or the modern EE and BT period, our team has the specialist knowledge to protect your interests. We have guided hundreds of landlords through EE lease renewals and understand the corporate, commercial, and legal landscape better than any general practice surveyor could.

Contact The Phone Mast Advice Company today on 01691 791543 for a free, no-obligation consultation. We have offices in London, Bristol, and Oswestry, and we act for landlords across the whole of the United Kingdom.

An O2 mast lease is rarely as simple as it first appears. What began as a straightforward agreement with a recognisable British mobile brand has, over two decades of corporate restructuring, become entangled in a web of parent companies, joint ventures, infrastructure partnerships, and ownership transfers that can leave even experienced property professionals struggling to keep pace. If you are a landlord with an O2 installation on your land or building, understanding exactly who holds your lease, who manages the site, and who will be sitting across the table at renewal is not merely useful — it is essential to protecting your rental income and your legal rights.

The Phone Mast Advice Company specialises exclusively in representing landlords in telecoms lease matters. We have never acted for an operator, and that independence is fundamental to the advice we give. Our team of specialist surveyors has navigated the full span of O2’s corporate evolution, from the BT Cellnet era through the Telefónica acquisition, the Cornerstone joint venture, the Virgin Media merger, and the more recent infrastructure divestments. We know how O2 structures its lease negotiations, what its agents are authorised to agree, and where the opportunities lie for landlords who are properly advised.

O2 in the UK: From BT Cellnet to Virgin Media O2

The story of O2 in Britain is one of constant transformation, and each chapter has left its mark on the thousands of mast leases scattered across the country. Tracing this history is more than an academic exercise — the corporate changes directly determine who you are dealing with today and what rights they hold under your lease.

O2’s origins lie in BT Cellnet, the mobile telephony arm of British Telecom. Launched in 1985, BT Cellnet was one of the first two cellular networks in the UK and built out an extensive portfolio of mast sites during the analogue and early digital eras. Many of the leases signed during this period remain in force today, and some still reference BT Cellnet or even British Telecommunications plc as the tenant — a source of considerable confusion for landlords trying to establish who they should be corresponding with.

In 2001, British Telecom demerged its mobile operations into a separate entity, and in 2002 the business was rebranded as O2. The new identity signalled a deliberate break from BT, and O2 quickly established itself as one of the UK’s most prominent consumer brands, winning the naming rights to the Millennium Dome in Greenwich (now The O2) and building a reputation for customer service that set it apart from its competitors.

The next major shift came in 2006, when the Spanish telecommunications giant Telefónica acquired O2 for approximately £17.7 billion. This brought O2 under the umbrella of one of Europe’s largest telecoms groups and introduced a layer of overseas corporate governance that would have lasting implications for how the UK mast estate was managed. Leases signed after the acquisition may reference Telefónica UK Limited rather than O2, adding another variant to the documentation landlords need to interpret.

In 2012, O2 and Vodafone established Cornerstone Telecommunications Infrastructure Limited (CTIL), a fifty-fifty joint venture that would take over the management and operation of both operators’ passive tower infrastructure. This was one of the most significant structural changes in the history of UK mobile infrastructure, consolidating more than 18,000 sites under a single operational entity. For landlords, it meant that the party knocking on the door about access, maintenance, or lease renewal was increasingly likely to be a Cornerstone representative rather than someone from O2 directly.

The blocked merger attempt of 2015-2016 is also worth noting. CK Hutchison, the owner of Three UK, launched a £10.25 billion bid to acquire O2 from Telefónica, which would have created the UK’s largest mobile operator a full decade before the eventual Vodafone-Three combination. The European Commission blocked the deal on competition grounds, and O2 remained under Telefónica’s ownership — but the episode signalled the industry’s direction of travel toward consolidation.

O2 launched its 5G network in 2019, initially in six UK cities, and began upgrading existing macro sites with new equipment to support the faster technology. For landlords, 5G upgrades often trigger lease provisions around additional apparatus and structural loading, creating both obligations and opportunities that require careful professional assessment.

The most transformative recent event was the merger of O2 with Virgin Media in June 2021, creating Virgin Media O2 (VMO2) as a fifty-fifty joint venture between Telefónica and Liberty Global. This was not a simple rebranding — it brought together a major mobile network and the UK’s largest cable broadband provider into a single commercial entity. The merged company operates under both the O2 and Virgin Media brands, and landlords may now receive correspondence bearing either name or the combined Virgin Media O2 identity.

Since the VMO2 merger, the corporate picture has continued to evolve. In 2023 and 2024, VMO2 began divesting a portion of its Cornerstone stake to GLIL Infrastructure, a consortium of local government pension funds. This partial disposal introduces yet another party with a financial interest in the mast infrastructure on your land. Additionally, in 2024, VMO2 extended a major mast-sharing agreement with Cellnex, one of Europe’s largest independent tower companies, covering more than 4,500 co-location sites across the United Kingdom. The practical consequence for landlords is that an O2 site may now involve operational input from Cornerstone, investment oversight from GLIL, and co-location management from Cellnex — all while the lease itself names a Telefónica subsidiary as tenant.

O2’s Mast Infrastructure Today

The scale of O2’s mast estate is substantial. Through Cornerstone, O2 has access to approximately 14,200 macro cell sites across the United Kingdom, encompassing ground-based tower installations, rooftop masts, and building-mounted antenna arrays. The Cellnex co-location agreement adds a further 4,500-plus sites where O2 equipment is hosted on infrastructure owned or managed by a third party.

What makes O2’s infrastructure particularly challenging for landlords is the trend toward reduced direct ownership. Virgin Media O2 has been systematically transferring operational and economic interests in tower assets to external parties — first through the Cornerstone JV, then through the GLIL stake sale, and now through the expanded Cellnex arrangement. Each transfer creates a potential disconnect between the entity named on your lease, the entity that actually manages the site, and the entity that ultimately profits from the infrastructure on your land. Navigating this requires specialist knowledge that goes well beyond conventional property surveying.

Navigating O2 Lease Complexities

When a landlord instructs The Phone Mast Advice Company to handle an O2 lease matter, we bring a depth of understanding of these corporate structures that is simply not available from generalist surveyors or solicitors. We are experienced telecoms surveyors who provide detailed rental assessments and negotiate the contractual terms landlords need during O2 lease renewals, collaborating with specialist telecom solicitors to manage lease completion from start to finish.

Our approach begins with establishing precisely who holds your lease and who is operationally responsible for the site. This is not always obvious, and getting it wrong can lead to wasted time, misdirected correspondence, and missed deadlines. We maintain current intelligence on O2’s corporate structure and the operational responsibilities of each entity in the chain, allowing us to identify the correct counterparty immediately and engage with the right people from the outset.

We then conduct a thorough rental assessment. This is not a desktop estimate based on generic assumptions — it is a detailed valuation drawing on our proprietary database of telecoms lease comparables from across the United Kingdom. We analyse the specific characteristics of your site, including its location, the type of installation, the alternative site availability in the area, and the terms of your existing lease, to arrive at a robust rental figure that we can defend in negotiation.

From there, we manage the entire negotiation process on your behalf. We correspond with Cornerstone, VMO2, Cellnex, or whichever entity is handling the matter, and we negotiate not only the headline rent but every material contractual term: lease duration, break provisions, sharing and upgrade rights, access arrangements, reinstatement obligations, and compensation mechanisms.

Key Issues in O2 Lease Negotiations

Several recurring themes arise in our O2 lease casework that landlords should be aware of.

Multiple Corporate Entities

As outlined above, an O2 mast site may involve Telefónica UK Limited, O2 (UK) Limited, Virgin Media O2, Cornerstone, GLIL Infrastructure, and Cellnex — sometimes several of these simultaneously. Each entity has its own commercial priorities, and correspondence may arrive from different parties at different stages of the process. Without a specialist adviser who understands these relationships, landlords can find themselves confused about who they are actually negotiating with and what authority that party has.

Changing Ownership Structures

O2’s progressive divestment of direct infrastructure ownership has implications for lease continuity. When interests are transferred between corporate entities, questions arise about whether the new party is bound by the terms of the original lease, whether notice provisions have been properly observed, and whether the landlord’s consent was required for any assignment. These are technical legal and surveying questions that demand specialist attention.

Rent Review Mechanisms

O2 leases contain a variety of rent review mechanisms depending on when they were granted. Older BT Cellnet-era leases may have fixed uplift provisions or RPI-linked reviews, while more recent agreements may specify open market rent reviews or reference the Electronic Communications Code valuation framework. Understanding which mechanism applies to your lease — and how to maximise your position under it — is central to achieving the right rental outcome.

The Electronic Communications Code versus the 1954 Act

Whether your O2 lease falls under the Electronic Communications Code or the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954 has profound implications for your rights and the rent you can expect. Code agreements are subject to a specific valuation methodology that tends to produce lower rents, while 1954 Act leases offer landlords stronger protections and a more favourable valuation basis. Determining which regime applies — and ensuring that the operator does not wrongly claim Code jurisdiction — is a critical part of our work.

How We Protect O2 Landlords’ Interests

Our services for O2 landlords cover every aspect of the mast lease lifecycle. We provide:

  • Lease Renewals — Comprehensive management of the renewal process, from initial assessment and strategy through negotiation of all commercial and contractual terms to liaison with solicitors for lease completion.
  • Rent Reviews — Rigorous rental assessments using comparable evidence from our national database, followed by structured negotiation to achieve the best achievable rent under your specific review mechanism.
  • New Site Lettings — If O2, Cornerstone, or Cellnex approaches you about a new installation, we ensure that the proposed rent reflects genuine market value and that the lease terms adequately protect your long-term interests.
  • Corporate Structure Analysis — Identification of all parties with an interest in your site, clarification of the lease-holding entity, and verification that any assignments or transfers have been properly executed.
  • Lease Sales — Independent valuation advice if you are considering selling your mast lease income stream, including assessment of offers from lease purchase companies.
  • Removal and Redevelopment Advice — If you need the O2 installation removed, whether for redevelopment, change of use, or any other reason, we advise on your options and negotiate the process with the operator.
  • Lease Retrieval — Obtaining copies of your lease documentation from the Land Registry or other sources if your records are incomplete, and providing a plain-English summary of the key terms.

Frequently Asked Questions About O2 Mast Leases

What is the difference between an O2 lease and a Virgin Media O2 lease?

The distinction is primarily one of corporate identity and timing. Leases granted before June 2021 will typically name O2 (UK) Limited or Telefónica UK Limited as the tenant. Following the merger with Virgin Media, new leases or renewals may reference Virgin Media O2 or one of its subsidiaries. The practical rights and obligations are broadly similar, but the change in named tenant can affect assignment provisions, guarantor arrangements, and the landlord’s ability to trace corporate liability. We review the specific documentation in each case to establish exactly where responsibilities lie.

My lease says Telefónica — is that the same as O2?

Telefónica UK Limited is the parent company of O2 in the United Kingdom and has been since Telefónica’s acquisition of O2 in 2006. Many leases name Telefónica UK Limited rather than O2 as the tenant, particularly those signed or renewed after the acquisition. For all practical purposes, a lease with Telefónica UK Limited relates to O2 infrastructure, but the precise corporate identity matters when it comes to enforcement, correspondence, and any future assignment or transfer of the lease. We always verify the registered details and confirm the current status of the tenant entity.

How does O2’s stake in Cornerstone affect my lease renewal?

O2’s interest in Cornerstone is held through the VMO2 joint venture, and a portion has been sold to GLIL Infrastructure. This means that the party negotiating your lease renewal — typically a Cornerstone representative — is acting on behalf of a complex ownership structure with multiple stakeholders. This can slow decision-making and create opportunities for the operator’s side to deflect or delay. Our role is to maintain pressure and focus on achieving a timely and fair outcome regardless of the internal corporate dynamics on the other side.

Can O2 assign my lease to Cellnex without my consent?

Whether O2 can assign your lease to Cellnex or any other party depends entirely on the terms of your specific lease agreement. Many older telecoms leases contain provisions that either prohibit assignment without the landlord’s consent or are silent on the matter. More recent leases, particularly those drafted under the Electronic Communications Code, may contain broader assignment rights. If you have received notice of an intended assignment, or if you suspect that your lease has been transferred without proper notice, you should seek specialist advice immediately. We can review your lease, assess whether any assignment has been lawfully executed, and advise you on your options.

What should I do if O2 is offering a lower rent at renewal?

A reduced rental offer at renewal is now standard practice across the telecoms industry, and O2’s agents through Cornerstone are no exception. The initial offer is almost always significantly below what can be achieved through professional negotiation. Before responding to any offer, we strongly recommend obtaining an independent rental assessment from a specialist telecoms surveyor. We will analyse the offer against current market evidence, assess the strength of the operator’s position, and negotiate on your behalf to secure a rent that properly reflects the value of your site. In many cases, we achieve outcomes that are multiples of the initial offer.

How does the Virgin Media O2 merger affect existing mast leases?

The formation of Virgin Media O2 in June 2021 did not automatically alter the terms of existing mast leases. Your lease remains a binding agreement with the named tenant, and the rent, duration, and other terms continue as before unless and until the lease is formally renewed or varied. However, the merger has had indirect effects. VMO2’s combined infrastructure strategy may result in changes to which sites are prioritised for investment, upgrade, or potential decommissioning. The integration of Virgin Media’s fixed-line network with O2’s mobile network also creates new demand for certain site types, particularly those suitable for small cells and fibre backhaul connections. We advise our clients on how these strategic shifts may affect their individual sites and how to position themselves advantageously in any forthcoming negotiation.

Speak to Our Team About Your O2 Lease Today

Dealing with an O2, Virgin Media O2, or Cornerstone lease does not have to be a source of frustration. With the right specialist representation, you can navigate the corporate complexity, secure a fair rent, and ensure that your lease terms properly protect your interests for years to come.

The Phone Mast Advice Company has offices in London, Bristol, and Oswestry, and we advise landlords with O2 mast installations throughout England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland. Call us on 01691 791543 for an initial discussion about your lease, or take advantage of our free lease check service to receive a professional assessment of your current position at no cost.

You can also reach us through our contact page. Whether your lease is due for renewal, you have received an offer you are unsure about, or you simply want to understand your options, our specialist telecoms surveyors are ready to help.
